content.body: Gaseous exchange is the process by which oxygen passes from the air into the blood and carbon dioxide passes from the blood into the air. It occurs in the alveoli of the lungs.
THE HUMAN RESPIRATORY SYSTEM: Air enters through the nose → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli (air sacs).

ALVEOLI: Tiny air sacs in the lungs. Ideal for gaseous exchange because they have: (1) thin walls (one cell thick), (2) large surface area (millions of alveoli), (3) moist lining, (4) rich blood supply (capillary network), (5) short diffusion path.

Inhaled air: ~21% O₂, 0.04% CO₂, 78% N₂
Exhaled air: ~16% O₂, 4% CO₂, 78% N₂

MECHANISM: Oxygen diffuses from alveolar air (high O₂) into blood capillaries (low O₂). Carbon dioxide diffuses from blood (high CO₂) into alveolar air (low CO₂). This is passive diffusion down concentration gradients.

questions.question: Gaseous exchange in humans occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Alveoli are the tiny air sacs where O₂ and CO₂ are exchanged between air and blood.

questions.question: Oxygen moves into blood from alveoli by:
questions.explanation: O₂ diffuses from high concentration in alveolar air to low concentration in blood — no energy needed.

questions.question: Compared to inhaled air, exhaled air has:
questions.explanation: Cells use oxygen and produce CO₂. Exhaled air: ~16% O₂ (down from 21%) and ~4% CO₂ (up from 0.04%).

questions.question: Which feature of alveoli is NOT an adaptation for gaseous exchange?
questions.explanation: Alveoli have ONE-CELL-THICK (very thin) walls for fast diffusion — thick walls would slow exchange.

questions.question: The percentage of CO₂ in exhaled air is approximately:
questions.explanation: Exhaled air contains about 4% CO₂, compared to only 0.04% in fresh air.

questions.question: The trachea is kept open by:
questions.explanation: C-shaped rings of cartilage prevent the trachea from collapsing during breathing.

questions.question: CO₂ moves from blood to alveolar air because:
options: Blood has less CO₂ than alveolar air
Blood has more CO₂ than alveolar air
questions.answer: Blood has more CO₂ than alveolar air
questions.explanation: CO₂ diffuses down its concentration gradient: from high (blood) to low (alveolar air).

questions.question: Why must alveolar surfaces be moist?
options: Gases must dissolve in water before diffusing across
questions.answer: Gases must dissolve in water before diffusing across
questions.explanation: O₂ and CO₂ dissolve in the moisture lining of alveoli before diffusing across the membrane.

questions.question: The large surface area of alveoli is due to:
questions.explanation: Human lungs have ~300 million alveoli, giving a total surface area of about 70m² — size of a tennis court.

questions.question: Nitrogen in inhaled and exhaled air is:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen is not used or produced by the body — its percentage stays at ~78% in both inhaled and exhaled air.

questions.question: The order of structures air passes through is:
questions.explanation: Air enters nose → trachea → two bronchi (one per lung) → bronchioles (smaller branches) → alveoli.

questions.question: Blood capillaries around alveoli are important because:
options: They provide short diffusion path and carry O₂ away quickly
questions.answer: They provide short diffusion path and carry O₂ away quickly
questions.explanation: Capillaries are one cell thick and wrap around alveoli. O₂ diffuses through just two cell layers.

questions.question: Gas exchange depends on:
questions.explanation: Diffusion occurs down concentration gradients — always from high to low concentration.

questions.question: During exercise, breathing rate increases because:
options: More O₂ is needed and more CO₂ must be removed
questions.answer: More O₂ is needed and more CO₂ must be removed
questions.explanation: Active muscles respire faster, consuming more O₂ and producing more CO₂ — breathing rate rises to meet demand.

questions.question: Emphysema (a lung disease) destroys alveolar walls. This reduces:
questions.explanation: Fewer, larger sacs replace many tiny alveoli — less surface area means less efficient gaseous exchange.

questions.question: Which gas diffuses from alveoli into blood?
questions.explanation: O₂ moves: alveoli→blood. CO₂ moves: blood→alveoli. These are opposite directions.

questions.question: The thin wall of alveoli is approximately:
questions.explanation: Alveolar walls are a single layer of squamous (flat) epithelial cells, minimising diffusion distance.

questions.question: Diaphragm and intercostal muscles are used for:
questions.explanation: These muscles create pressure changes that move air in and out, keeping fresh air in alveoli.

questions.question: Which breath test detects CO₂ in exhaled air?
questions.explanation: CO₂ + Ca(OH)₂ (lime water) → CaCO₃ (white precipitate). Exhaled air turns lime water milky.

questions.question: The main purpose of gaseous exchange is to:
options: Supply O₂ for respiration and remove CO₂
questions.answer: Supply O₂ for respiration and remove CO₂
questions.explanation: Cells need O₂ for aerobic respiration (energy release). CO₂ is a waste product that must be removed.

lessons.title: Gaseous Exchange in Plants and Fish

content.body: Different organisms have different surfaces and organs for gaseous exchange adapted to their environment.

PLANTS: Gas exchange occurs mainly through stomata (tiny pores) on leaves. Guard cells control opening and closing of stomata.
During the day: CO₂ enters for photosynthesis; O₂ exits.
During respiration (always): O₂ enters; CO₂ exits.
FISH: Use gills for gaseous exchange. Water flows over gill filaments (lamellae). Blood flows in opposite direction to water (counter-current flow) — maximises O₂ extraction efficiency.

Leaf stomata: Open in light (guard cells take in water, swell → pore opens)
Close in dark/drought (guard cells lose water, shrink → pore closes)

content.title: Counter-current in Fish Gills
content.body:
Water (high O₂) flows one way. Blood (low O₂) flows opposite direction.
At all points, blood O₂ < water O₂ → diffusion continues along full length of gill.
Result: ~80% of O₂ extracted from water.

questions.question: Gas exchange in plants occurs mainly through:
questions.explanation: Stomata are tiny pores on leaf surfaces (mostly underside) through which gases diffuse.

questions.question: Guard cells control:
questions.explanation: Guard cells change shape depending on water content, opening and closing the stomatal pore.

questions.question: Stomata open during the day because:
options: Guard cells absorb water and swell, pulling pore open
questions.answer: Guard cells absorb water and swell, pulling pore open
questions.explanation: In light, guard cells take up water by osmosis, become turgid and curved, pulling the pore open.

questions.question: Fish exchange gases using:
questions.explanation: Gills are the gaseous exchange organs of fish, extracting dissolved O₂ from water.

questions.question: Counter-current flow in fish gills means:
options: Water and blood flow same direction
Water and blood flow in opposite directions
questions.answer: Water and blood flow in opposite directions
questions.explanation: Counter-current maximises diffusion by keeping a concentration gradient along the full length of the gill.

questions.question: During the day, plants absorb CO₂ for:
questions.explanation: Plants use CO₂ + water + light energy in photosynthesis to make glucose and O₂.

questions.question: At night, plants:
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis requires light. At night, only respiration occurs — plants take in O₂ and release CO₂.

questions.question: Gill filaments (lamellae) in fish are adapted for gas exchange because they:
options: Have large surface area and rich blood supply
questions.answer: Have large surface area and rich blood supply
questions.explanation: Many thin lamellae increase surface area; dense capillaries provide short diffusion path.

questions.question: Which gas enters stomata for photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide is the raw material for photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

questions.question: Most stomata are found on the:
questions.explanation: Lower surface is cooler and shaded — reduces water loss. This is where most stomata are located.

questions.question: Advantage of counter-current flow in gills over parallel flow:
questions.explanation: Counter-current maintains gradient along full gill length. Parallel flow quickly equalises and diffusion stops.

questions.question: Water enters a fish's mouth and exits through:
questions.explanation: Fish pump water: mouth → over gills (gas exchange) → out through gill slits on sides.

questions.question: Stomata close during drought to:
questions.explanation: When water is scarce, guard cells lose water and stomata close — reducing transpiration (water loss).

questions.question: In both plant leaves and fish gills, gaseous exchange depends on:
questions.explanation: All gaseous exchange relies on diffusion — gases dissolve in moisture and move down concentration gradients.

questions.question: Spongy mesophyll cells in leaves have air spaces to:
options: Allow gas movement within the leaf
questions.answer: Allow gas movement within the leaf
questions.explanation: Air spaces between spongy cells allow CO₂ and O₂ to circulate freely inside the leaf to cells.

questions.question: When O₂ is released by plants during photosynthesis, it exits through:
questions.explanation: O₂ produced in chloroplasts diffuses out of leaf cells and through open stomata.

questions.question: A fish moved to water with less dissolved oxygen would:
options: Increase the rate of water pumping over gills
questions.answer: Increase the rate of water pumping over gills
questions.explanation: Less O₂ per volume of water → pump more water to get enough O₂. Like breathing faster when air is thin.

questions.question: What happens to stomata when guard cells become flaccid (lose water)?
questions.explanation: Flaccid guard cells become straight and close the pore between them.

questions.question: Aquatic plants get CO₂ for photosynthesis from:
questions.explanation: CO₂ from water enters through thin cell walls of aquatic plants; some also absorbs from air at surface.

questions.question: The gas exchange surface of fish gills and human alveoli share what feature?
options: Large surface area, thin walls, moist, rich blood supply
questions.answer: Large surface area, thin walls, moist, rich blood supply
questions.explanation: All effective gas exchange surfaces share these four features regardless of organism.
